main_sections.title: The Cherry Tree
main_sections.prose_summary: The Cherry Tree is a tender narrative by Ruskin Bond that traces the bond between a young boy, Rakesh, and a cherry tree he grows from a single seed over several years in the Himalayan foothills near Mussoorie. Rakesh, who lives with his grandfather while attending school, walks home one day eating cherries bought from the Mussoorie bazaar. On his grandfather's gentle prompting that luck must be put to use, he plants the last cherry seed in a soft, shaded corner of the garden. He then forgets about it entirely, absorbed in the rhythms of school and cricket. Spring brings the first surprise — a tiny rooted shoot that Rakesh mistakes for a twig before joyfully calling his grandfather. The two bend together in wonder over a four-inch sapling. Seasons accumulate around the growing tree: monsoon rains speed its growth to two feet, only for a wayward goat to strip all its leaves; a grass-cutter's scythe slices it in two; field-mice cluster in the cottage roof under winter snow while the tree bends low under its weight. Yet each time the cherry tree recovers, sending out new shoots with quiet determination, mirroring Rakesh's own steady growth from a six-year-old boy to a sturdy nine-year-old. One February morning, grandfather spots the first pale pink blossom and calls out in disbelief and delight. In subsequent years blossoms multiply; bees feed on nectar; bulbuls and scarlet minivets feast on small cherries; a praying-mantis and a hairy caterpillar arrive as uninvited guests. Eventually grandfather finds the perfect shade of the fully grown tree, reclining in a cane chair and watching leaves dance in the breeze, while Rakesh lies beneath it gazing up at the blue sky and distant mountains. In the dusk, with crickets and cicadas filling the forest, Rakesh asks why this tree is special among so many. His grandfather answers simply: 'We planted it ourselves.' Touching the smooth bark, Rakesh whispers, 'Is this what it feels to be God?' The story celebrates patience, the quiet triumph of nature, and the deep human satisfaction of nurturing life from a single seed.

reading_for_comprehension.section_name: Let us discuss + Let us think and reflect

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Arrange the events in order of occurrence in the story. Two have been done for you. Share your answers with your classmates and the teacher.

question_bank.stem: Rakesh discovered that the small, well-rooted twig, had grown into a cherry tree.
Rakesh was sent to live with his grandfather in Mussoorie.
Rakesh pressed the cherry seed into the soft soil with his thumb.
The cherry tree grew quickly after the rains arrived early.
Rakesh bought a bunch of cherries and ate most of them.
A goat entered the garden and ate all the leaves of the young cherry tree.
Rakesh's parents grew maize, rice, and barley in a village. (1)
Rakesh sprinkled water on the cherry tree and surrounded it with pebbles.
Grandfather told Rakesh that cherry trees were tough.
Rakesh kept rolling the last cherry seed in his mouth.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Do you think the cherry tree would survive? Why do you think so?
Complete the table given below based on your understanding of Part II of the story. One example has been done for you. Share your answers with your classmates and teacher.

question_bank.stem: A woman cut the cherry tree while cutting grass. → (i) Grandfather scolded the woman.
question_bank.table:
Cause|Effect
A woman cut the cherry tree while cutting grass.|(i) Grandfather scolded the woman.
2.|(ii) Rakesh was thinner but stronger when he came back.
A hairy caterpillar started eating the leaves of the cherry tree.|(iii)
4.|(iv) Rakesh and Grandfather gazed at the tree as though it was a miracle.
Rakesh tasted one of the small cherries during summer.|(v)

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
Read the given extracts and answer the questions that follow.

Extract 1: "Have a cherry, Grandfather," he said, as soon as he saw his grandfather in the garden. Grandfather took one cherry and Rakesh promptly ate the other two. He kept the last seed in his mouth for some time, rolling it round and round on his tongue until all the tang had gone. Then he placed the seed on the palm of his hand and studied it. "Are cherry seeds lucky?" asked Rakesh. "Of course." "Then I'll keep it." "Nothing is lucky if you put it away. If you want luck, you must put it to some use."

question_bank.stem: Substitute the underlined phrase with a correct word from the extract. The Kho-kho captain changed her strategy quickly and without delay when a new player came in from the opposing team.

What does Rakesh's action of rolling the cherry seed in his mouth most likely suggest about his mood at that moment? A. He is feeling impatient and restless. B. He is enjoying the taste and the moment. C. He is bored and has nothing better to do. D. He is thoughtful and feeling playful.
options: He is feeling impatient and restless.
He is enjoying the taste and the moment.
He is bored and has nothing better to do.
He is thoughtful and feeling playful.

question_bank.stem: Complete the following sentence suitably with reference to Rakesh and his grandfather. Rakesh's decision to plant the seed shows that ______________ . (he respects and values his grandfather's advice / he has a close bond with his grandfather and a willingness to learn from him)
How does the grandfather's advice reflect his view about luck and effort?

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
Read the given extract and answer the questions that follow.

Extract 2: Winter came early. The cherry tree bent low with the weight of snow. Field-mice sought shelter in the roof of the cottage. The road from the valley was blocked, and for several days there was no newspaper, and this made Grandfather quite grumpy. His stories began to have unhappy endings. In February it was Rakesh's birthday. He was nine—and the tree was four, but almost as tall as Rakesh.

question_bank.stem: Complete the following sentence suitably. The phrase 'field-mice sought shelter in the roof of the cottage' suggests that _____________.

What can be inferred about the cherry tree's toughness from the line, 'The cherry tree bent low with the weight of snow'? A. The weight of the tree was more than the snow. B. The tree could withstand harsh weather. C. The tree was growing close to the ground. D. The snow gave support to the tree.
options: The weight of the tree was more than the snow.
The tree could withstand harsh weather.
The tree was growing close to the ground.
The snow gave support to the tree.

question_bank.stem: How did the early winter affect Grandfather's mood and storytelling?

Complete the sentence by choosing the correct option. The comparison of Rakesh's age to the cherry tree's age symbolises _______________. (the tree's growth paralleling Rakesh's own development / the tree standing as a dear one on Rakesh's birthday)

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Answer the following questions.

question_bank.stem: Why did Rakesh's parents send him to Mussoorie?
Why did Rakesh let the praying-mantis remain on the branch of the cherry tree?
How is life in the Himalayan foothills described in the story?
What might Rakesh mean when he says, "Is this what it feels to be God?" at the end of the story?
How does the writer describe the relationship between Rakesh and his grandfather?
What can you infer about the connection between humans and nature based on the story?
Imagine Rakesh reflects on his wonderful experience of watching a seed being transformed into a full-grown cherry tree. Write how he must have felt.

listening.transcript_text:
SON: Amma, could you help me with this project?
MOTHER: Why not? What's the project about?
SON: It's about the Chipko Movement. I have to make a PowerPoint presentation and talk about it in class.
MOTHER: That's good! Chipko Movement was all about hugging trees to prevent them from being cut, isn't it? Such an inspiring movement it was!
SON: Yes, it was the first time that common people came together for the noble cause of saving trees. It showed how people can also make a difference. Have you heard or read about something similar?
MOTHER: Yes, of course. The success of the Chipko Movement led to the Appiko Movement.
SON: Really? Doesn't appiko mean 'hugging' in Kannada? So, did they also hug trees to protect them?
MOTHER: Yes they did. In this movement people not only hugged trees to protect them but also regenerated the forest areas in the Western Ghats.
SON: That's great! This reminds me of what my teacher shared about Saalumarada Thimakka last week. She is a Padma Shri awardee.
MOTHER: What an amazing personality! I've heard about her and I'm interested to know what your teacher has shared with you.
SON: She is an Indian environmentalist who has been planting trees since 1948. She has planted over 8000 trees in the area around her village.
MOTHER: Wonderful!
SON: What I admire about her is the commitment she has towards conserving the environment.
MOTHER: Incredible! That is one superwoman! But do you know who the Forest Man of India is?
SON: Yes, I've read about him. It is Jadav Payeng, from Assam. He too is a Padma Shri awardee. Isn't he the one who single-handedly turned a barren land of around a thousand acres into a lush green forest? He did so by planting nearly forty million trees.
MOTHER: Yes, you're absolutely right. It was not something that happened overnight. It took him thirty years to achieve this. This forest has become a habitat for different kinds of animals including migratory birds.
SON: Fascinating, isn't it? I've read a book and watched a documentary in school about him which has really inspired me.
MOTHER: I'm glad that you have begun to care about the environment too.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: You will listen to a conversation between a son and his mother. As you listen, select four true statements from 1–7 given below.

question_bank.stem: The mother encouraged her son to deliver a presentation on the Chipko Movement.
The son was surprised to know about the Appiko Movement.
The son agreed that the Chipko Movement was about people uniting to make an impact.
The mother expressed a desire to share more details about Saalumarada Thimakka.
The mother was curious to hear about Saalumarada Thimakka.
The mother and son were in disagreement about the work of Jadav Payeng.
The son found the information on Jadav Payeng wonderful.

Speak about Van Mahotsav Diwas and its significance. Before you speak in the class or assembly, prepare yourself on the following points: When is it celebrated? Who celebrates it? Why is it celebrated? How is it celebrated? What is the message it conveys?

Complete the article for a magazine on 'Trees–Our Lifeline'. Use the words and phrases given in the box below: absorb carbon dioxide, Moreover, our planet, reduces pollution, Additionally, would stop existing, ensures a better future, climate control, Trees–Our Lifeline, To conclude, If trees were not there. Fill in the blanks (i) to (x) to complete the article.

question_bank.stem: Fill in blank (i): Title of the article
Fill in blank (ii): Without them, life as we know ________________.
Fill in blank (iii): Trees ________________ and release oxygen, helping us breathe.
Fill in blank (iv): This process also ________________ and keeps the environment clean.
Fill in blank (v): ________________, trees give us fruits, nuts, and other foods.
Fill in blank (vi): Trees also help in ________________ and prevention of soil erosion.
Fill in blank (vii): ________________, the air would be polluted, and we would struggle to breathe.
Fill in blank (viii): ________________ would be barren, lacking food, shelter, and life-giving greenery.
Fill in blank (ix): ________________, trees are true friends.
Fill in blank (x): Protecting and planting trees ________________ for everyone on Earth.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read the following lines from the text and underline all the verbs and identify the form of tense. 1. Rakesh was on his way home from school when he bought the cherries. 2. Rakesh was looking for Grandfather without finding him in any of his favourite places around the house. 3. Rakesh and Grandfather gazed at the tree as though it had performed a miracle. The forms of tenses are Simple Past Tense (1 and 3), Past Progressive Tense (2), and Past Perfect Tense (3).

question_bank.stem: Identify the tense forms in the given sentences.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Now, fill in the blanks with the correct form of Simple Past, Past Progressive, or Past Perfect Tenses of the verbs given in brackets. (i) I was sitting (sit) with my grandparents in the garden… As we (ii) ______________ (talk), I (iii) ______________ (realise) how much wisdom they (iv) ______________ (impart) to me over the years. While I (v) ______________ (listen) to their stories for hours together, I (vi) ______________ (remember) the times we (vii) ______________ (spend) together planting flowers and learning about life. By the time I left, the sun (viii) ______________ (set), and I (ix) ______________ (feel) a deep sense of gratitude for the bond we (x) ______________ (nurture) through love, care, and countless memories.

question_bank.stem: Fill in blank (ii): As we ______________ (talk)
Fill in blank (iii): I ______________ (realise)
Fill in blank (iv): they ______________ (impart)
Fill in blank (v): While I ______________ (listen)
Fill in blank (vi): I ______________ (remember)
Fill in blank (vii): the times we ______________ (spend)
Fill in blank (viii): the sun ______________ (set)

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Study the punctuation marks in the following sentences from the text. Now, punctuate the following paragraph appropriately: 'every sunday roshni would visit her grandparents house on their farm come sit grandpa would say patting the bench on their porch roshni loved these moments where the world slowed down and stories flowed like magic grandma would bring tea her eyes twinkling with joy im excited to hear about your younger days the adventures the lessons and the memories roshni said eagerly grandpa smiled softly his wrinkled hands resting on the cane ah the old days… when everything was simple and happiness was all we had roshni could feel the warmth of those memories he sat in silence bound not by words but by a timeless bond'

question_bank.stem: Punctuate the given paragraph appropriately using the rules learned.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Did you know that the banyan tree is the National Tree of India? Our country has age-old banyan trees. Some of them are: 1. The Great Banyan, located in Shibpur, Howrah, near Kolkata. 2. Dodda Aalada Mara, located in Kethohalli village, near Bengaluru. 3. Thimmamma Marrimanu, in Anantapur District in Andhra Pradesh. It was recorded as the largest tree specimen in the world in the Guinness Book of World Records in 1989.

Kailash Sankhala, 'The Tiger Man of India', was an Indian biologist and conservationist. He carried out a census of the tigers in India in 1971. Later, because of his studies, he was appointed the first director of Project Tiger—an initiative to prevent the extinction of the Indian Tiger. He was awarded the Padma Shri in 1992. Now, find more information about such Indian environmentalists and share with your classmates.

question_bank.stem: Find more information about Indian environmentalists and share with your classmates.

main_sections.title: Harvest Hymn
main_sections.poem_text:
Men's Voices:
Lord of the lotus, lord of the harvest,
Bright and munificent lord of the morn!
Thine is the bounty that prospered our sowing,
Thine is the bounty that nurtured our corn.
We bring thee our songs and our garlands for tribute,
The gold of our fields and the gold of our fruit;
O giver of mellowing radiance, we hail thee,
We praise thee, O Surya, with cymbal and flute.

Lord of the rainbow, lord of the harvest,
Great and beneficent lord of the main!
Thine is the mercy that cherished our furrows,
Thine is the mercy that fostered our grain.
We bring thee our thanks and our garlands for tribute,
The wealth of our valleys, new-garnered and ripe;
O sender of rain and the dewfall, we hail thee,
We praise thee, Varuna, with cymbal and pipe.

Women's Voices:
Queen of the gourd-flower, queen of the harvest,
Sweet and omnipotent mother, O Earth!
Thine is the plentiful bosom that feeds us,
Thine is the womb where our riches have birth.
We bring thee our love and our garlands for tribute,
With gifts of thy opulent giving we come;
O source of our manifold gladness, we hail thee,
We praise thee, O Prithvi, with cymbal and drum.

All Voices:
Lord of the Universe, Lord of our being,
Father eternal, ineffable Om!
Thou art the Seed and the Scythe of our harvests,
Thou art our Hands and our Heart and our Home.
We bring thee our lives and our labours for tribute,
Grant us thy succour, thy counsel, thy care.
O Life of all life and all blessing, we hail thee,
We praise thee, O Bramha, with cymbal and prayer.
main_sections.poem_appreciation_summary: Harvest Hymn by Sarojini Naidu is a devotional ode of gratitude offered by the farming community at harvest time. Structured as four stanzas sung by distinct voices — men, women, and all together — the hymn addresses three elemental deities: Surya (the Sun), Varuna (the Rain), and Prithvi (the Earth), before culminating in an address to Brahma, the universal creator. The central theme is the deep, reverent interdependence between farmers and the forces of nature that sustain life. The tone is celebratory and humble simultaneously — the speakers bring songs, garlands, the literal gold of their fields, and ultimately their own lives as tribute. Naidu employs rich imagery of light, moisture, seed, and harvest; anaphora ('Thine is... Thine is') creates a litany-like rhythm that reinforces collective devotion. The dominant metaphor in the final stanza — God as both Seed and Scythe — encapsulates the Hindu concept of the divine as the source and end of all creation. Personification of Earth as an all-nourishing mother is especially vivid. The poem's musical quality, achieved through repeated refrains ('we hail thee... we praise thee') and the mention of cymbals, flute, pipe, drum, and prayer, recreates the sensory atmosphere of a harvest festival.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Write the stanza number for the main idea of each stanza given below. Share your answers with your classmates and teacher.

question_bank.stem: The speakers recognise the lord's kindness and praise him for nourishing the field.
The speakers praise the generosity of the creator of the universe who ensures a rich harvest.
The speakers devote their lives and work as a mark of respect to the lord and seek his support, wisdom, and care.
The speakers pay homage to the Earth and appreciate her role in supporting life, by dedicating their harvest.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Fill in the blanks by choosing the appropriate words or phrases from within the brackets.

question_bank.stem: The setting of the poem is a rural landscape, possibly during a __________________ (harvest/religious) festival.
The tone of the poem is respectful and celebratory because it praises ___________________ (the men and women / nature and the Lord) with deep respect and gratitude.

In the line 'Thou art our Hands and our Heart and our Home', the letter 'h' is capitalised in 'Hands', 'Heart' and 'Home' because ___________________. (it represents the hard work of farmers / God resides in them)

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Identify the poetic device used in the following lines from the poem.

question_bank.stem: Bright and munificent lord of the morn!
We bring thee our lives and our labours for tribute

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Match the lines from the poem in Column 1 with their expressions in Column 2. Choose the appropriate poetic device from the box given below and write in Column 3. Devices: imagery, alliteration, simile, metaphor, personification.

question_bank.stem: Match: 'sweet and omnipotent mother' — (i) creates a clear description of the scene and appeals to the senses / (ii) implied comparison of growth and reaping with beginning and end of all life / (iii) the earth is being given human-like qualities
question_bank.table:
Line|Expression|Poetic Device
sweet and omnipotent mother|(i) creates a clear description of the scene and appeals to the senses|
The wealth of our valleys, new-garnered and ripe|(ii) implied comparison of growth and reaping with beginning and end of all life|
Thou art the Seed and the Scythe|(iii) the earth is being given human-like qualities|

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Certain words or phrases are repeated in the same line of the poem. For example, The gold of our fields and the gold of our fruit. Find more such repetitions from the poem. What is the poet's purpose of using repetition in the poem?
The poem uses symbolism to convey deeper meanings. Match the symbols in Column 1 with the meanings they convey in Column 2.

question_bank.stem: Match symbols to meanings: Prithvi (the Earth), Surya (the Sun), Varuna (Rain) with their symbolic meanings.
question_bank.table:
Symbol|Meaning
Prithvi (the Earth)|(i) light, energy, life—representing the giver of warmth and prosperity
Surya (the Sun)|(ii) mercy and nourishment—crucial for agriculture and sustenance
Varuna (Rain)|(iii) fertile land, motherhood, nurturing—the source of all life

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
Read the given extracts and answer the questions that follow.

Extract 1: We bring thee our songs and our garlands for tribute, / The gold of our fields and the gold of our fruit; / O giver of mellowing radiance, we hail thee, / We praise thee, O Surya, with cymbal and flute.

question_bank.stem: What does the act of bringing songs and garlands as tribute suggest about the people's relationship with the divine? A. They fear the gods and wish to calm them in any way possible. B. They have a deep sense of reverence and gratitude towards the divine. C. They are forced to perform rituals without understanding the divine. D. They depend on the gods for their daily needs and activities.
options: They fear the gods and wish to calm them in any way possible.
They have a deep sense of reverence and gratitude towards the divine.
They are forced to perform rituals without understanding the divine.
They depend on the gods for their daily needs and activities.

question_bank.stem: Complete the following sentence suitably. The word 'gold' in the phrases 'the gold of our fields' and 'the gold of our fruit' symbolises ________________.
How does the description 'O giver of mellowing radiance' help us understand Surya's role in the harvest?
Fill in the blank by selecting the correct option. The use of cymbal and flute signifies ________________. (joy and celebration / meditation and worship)

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
Read the given extract and answer the questions that follow.

Extract 2: All Voices: / Lord of the Universe, Lord of our being, / Father eternal, ineffable Om! / Thou art the Seed and the Scythe of our harvests, / Thou art our Hands and our Heart and our Home.

question_bank.stem: In the phrase 'Lord of our being,' what does the word 'being' most likely refer to? A. Our physical body B. Our entire existence C. Our spiritual practices D. Our earthly possessions

Identify whether the following statement is true or false. The imagery of 'the Seed and the Scythe' symbolises the divine's role as both the beginning (creation) and the end (harvest) of the agricultural cycle.
Identify the phrases from the extract that mean the following: A. The divine as a timeless, everlasting presence B. Beyond human comprehension or description
How do we know that these lines from the extract are spoken as a chorus?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Answer the following questions.

question_bank.stem: Why do the farmers hail the God of rain?
What is being referred to as 'wealth of our valleys'? Why?
State one reason why the women refer to the Earth as a 'source of manifold gladness'.
Why does everyone in the poem offer their 'labours for tribute'?
Why has the poet presented the poem as voices of men, women, and all voices?
How does the poet create the joyous atmosphere of rural India at harvesting time?
Why is the poem presented as a hymn?

listening.transcript_text: Hello everyone! Did you know that rice is actually a type of grass, and the part we eat is its seed? Let me tell you about the process of cultivating rice. It all starts with preparing the land. First, the soil has to be ploughed. Rice is typically grown in fertile soil that holds a lot of water. The most important step in preparing the soil is to soak it with plenty of water. This step is called puddling. Once the nursery bed is prepared, the seeds can be planted. After that, we sow the seeds on the soil's surface. Paddy farmers have to ensure that their fields stay flooded with water because irrigation is crucial. It takes around 4 to 5 weeks for the seeds to grow into seedlings. After this comes the most important step—transplanting. The seedlings that were prepared in the nursery bed are uprooted and then transplanted into the flooded field. In around 120 days, the rice plants are fully grown. At this point, the crops are harvested by cutting them. Following this, the seeds are separated from the plant. This is called threshing. To reduce the moisture content even more, the separated grains are dried. This ensures that they can be stored or processed further. Through a process called milling, the rice is separated from the outer covering on it. Finally, the rice reaches the market, and that's where we get it to make all kinds of delicious foods. Whether it's Appam, Pulao, Khichdi, or Daal Bhaat, rice is a big part of many meals.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Listen to the talk about paddy cultivation. As you listen, number the events in the correct order in which they happen.

question_bank.stem: Transplanting of seedlings
Ploughing of soil
Threshing of grains
Puddling of land
Harvesting of paddy
Irrigating the field
Milling of rice

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Now, listen to the talk again. As you listen, choose the correct option to answer the questions that follow.

question_bank.stem: What is the main step in preparing the soil for cultivation of rice? (i) Ploughing the soil (ii) Clearing the soil (iii) Puddling the soil
How long does it take for rice seeds to grow into seedlings before being transplanted? (i) 2–3 weeks (ii) 4–5 weeks (iii) 6–7 weeks
Why is drying rice grains important after threshing? (i) To get rice seeds for plantation (ii) To separate the rice grains easily (iii) To store the rice grains for longer
options: To get rice seeds for plantation
To separate the rice grains easily
To store the rice grains for longer

tasks_verbatim.task_text: In English, some letters are not pronounced in certain words. The letter 'n' is silent when it appears with 'm' at the end of the word: hymn, column, autumn, solemn. The letter 'b' is silent when it appears with 'm': womb, tomb, bomb, climb, comb. Now, say these words aloud.

Work in groups and make a presentation on any popular dish made during the harvest festival in your region. Include: What is the dish called? Is it known by any other name in other parts of India? What ingredients are used? How is the dish prepared? How is it served? What does the dish taste like? What is the special ritual related to the preparation? Describe any special memory associated with the preparation.

question_bank.stem: Prepare and deliver a group presentation on a popular harvest festival dish from your region using the given prompts.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Your school wishes to express gratitude to the farmers who play an important role in providing us the food on our plates. Draft a creative slogan to be put up on the school notice board. Remember to: focus on the main idea to be expressed, use simple language, be concise—express yourself in a few words. You may write the slogan on a chart paper and decorate it.

question_bank.stem: Draft a creative slogan expressing gratitude to farmers, to be displayed on the school notice board.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Match the pictures of the farming implements given in Column 1 with their use given in Column 2. Then, fill in the blanks in Column 3 to complete the name of the implement.

question_bank.stem: Match farming implement pictures with their uses and complete names: S__CKL__, SC__TH__, PL__GH, TR__W__L, R__K__, SP__D__
question_bank.table:
Implement Image|Use|Incomplete Name
1|(i) A tool with a long handle with metal teeth for collecting hay or leaves.|S__CKL__
2|(ii) A small hand tool used for digging and making shallow furrows.|SC__TH__
3|(iii) A hand-held tool with a curved blade attached to a short handle. Used for harvesting crops.|PL__GH
4|(iv) A tool pulled by oxen. Used for turning over the soil in the field so that seeds can be sown or planted.|TR__W__L
5|(v) A farm tool having a flat rectangular blade attached to a long wooden handle used for digging the soil.|R__K__
6|(vi) A hand-held tool with a long-curved blade attached to a long handle used for cutting tall grass.|SP__D__

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Choose the correct synonyms for the underlined words in the given sentences from the box below. Circle the words or phrases in the sentences that helped you to infer the meanings. Box: generous, help, brightness, indescribable, kind.

question_bank.stem: The king was beneficent to the poor because he took good care of them. synonym: kind (example done)
He was munificent towards the needy and donated a lot for their health care. synonym: ____________________
When he lost his job, all his friends gave him succour by supporting him with money. synonym: ____________________
When the sun's rays came into the dark room, the whole room was full of radiance. synonym: ____________________

main_sections.title: Waiting for the Rain
main_sections.prose_summary: Waiting for the Rain by Kamakshi Balasubramanian is a quietly philosophical narrative set in an unnamed village during a severe drought. Velu, a hard-working farmer who has cultivated his small piece of land for six years without a break, opens the story by scanning a cloudless sky, muttering that the day does not look encouraging. The text immediately establishes both his character — disciplined, rational, resistant to superstition — and his crisis: the rains have failed to come for months, the fields lie cracked and bare, and the farmers wait helplessly. When a neighbour suggests consulting astrologers, Velu firmly rejects the idea and instead makes a practical journey to the city weather office, only to be told by bemused forecasters that they themselves cannot explain the anomaly. Returning home tired and thirsty, Velu rests under a large tree and encounters an old woman, whose wrinkled skin and sparkling eyes suggest she is both aged and wise. She interprets his bitterness as an invitation to counsel him. At first she gently puzzles Velu by suggesting he may have worked 'too hard'; when he protests, she redirects his attention not to himself but to the land. The earth, she explains in a soft, smiling voice, is ancient — it has worked for thousands of years through every ploughing, sowing, and planting season. Just as human beings need rest, the land too needs a fallow pause, and when rain does not fall, nature is providing exactly that respite. The land lies undisturbed, resting and replenishing itself; and when the rains finally arrive, it will spring back to fertility with renewed vigour. Velu walks home slowly, turning this idea over in his mind, moving from skepticism toward an emerging peace. As he approaches the village in the evening, a cool breeze touches his back, a single drop lands on his shoulder, and clouds gather in the distance. Rain is coming. Velu runs home laughing. The story is an understated ecological parable about soil health, the wisdom of natural cycles, and the importance of listening to traditional knowledge, framed through the encounter between a rational modern farmer and an ancient voice of the earth.

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
Read the given extracts and answer the questions that follow.

Extract 1: "...I can't see how talking to astrologers will bring rain." He decided to go to the weather office in the city and talk to someone. But the people at the weather office said they couldn't really tell him when the sky would gather clouds and bring rain. "We are at a loss ourselves!" they exclaimed. "So many favourable conditions but still no rain. Very odd!"

question_bank.stem: What does the first line of the extract tell us about Velu's beliefs?

question_bank.stem: Complete the following sentence with a suitable option. The absence of rain despite favourable conditions, shows ___________________. A. lack of astrological knowledge B. ineffectiveness of farmers like Velu C. delay in understanding the weather D. unpredictability of nature

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
Read the given extract and answer the questions that follow.

Extract 2: "The soil, the land, the earth... shouldn't someone let the land rest a bit?" the old woman said, softly, smiling. "Rest? Let the land rest? I don't understand," Velu looked at her, a little puzzled. "Yes, my son, that is the difficulty. You don't realise that the earth is old..."

question_bank.stem: The old woman's advice was accompanied with a soft tone and a smile. What does that tell us about her?
Fill in the blank with the correct option from those given below. The old woman's advice ___________ Velu, at that moment. A. failed to impress B. confused C. scared D. made sense to
State one thing about the old woman when she referred to Velu as 'my son', while offering advice.
Complete the following sentence with a suitable explanation. The 'difficulty' that the old woman mentions is due to the fact that ________________.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Answer the following questions.

question_bank.stem: How does the old woman convey Nature's wisdom to Velu?
The writer says, 'The fields lay untended…' Why couldn't the farmers tend to their fields in the absence of rain?
Support the old woman's perspective that the land needs rest just like people do.
What can be inferred about Velu's character based on his reaction to the drought?
What does the story suggest about the life of farmers and their relationship with nature?
How might the rest and care of land contribute to a balanced and sustainable ecosystem?

listening.transcript_text: Hello and welcome to today's weather forecast. Delhi is expected to have a generally cloudy sky with light rain. The temperature will range between a minimum of 25 °C and a maximum of 36 °C. The sky will generally be cloudy with moderate rain in Mumbai. The minimum temperature is 24 °C and the maximum will be around 30 °C. Moving eastwards, Kolkata will have a partly cloudy sky with one or two spells of rain or thundershowers. The minimum temperature will be 28 °C and the maximum will be around 34 °C. Chennai is expected to have a partly cloudy sky with a possibility of thunderstorm. The minimum temperature will be 27 °C and the maximum will be around 36 °C. Bengaluru will have a generally cloudy sky with light rain. The minimum temperature will be 21 °C and will rise to a maximum of around 29 °C. Hyderabad is expected to have a generally cloudy sky with light rain or drizzle. Temperature will be between a minimum of 23 °C and a maximum of 33 °C. That's all from me today. Have a beautiful day ahead. Don't forget to carry your umbrellas!

Rain has arrived after a long dry spell on the Earth. Imagine the Rain and the Earth have a conversation. Work in pairs and present the role-play. You may start: Rain: Greetings, Earth! It's been a while since we've met. How have you been? Earth: Hello Rain! I am so glad to see you. Continue using the provided hints for Rain and Earth.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: You have observed the difficulties faced by the people in your neighbourhood due to scarcity of water especially during the summer months. Write a letter to the Councillor of your Municipal ward, requesting her/him to set up a rain water harvesting unit. Provide suggestions to address the issue. Remember to: use formal language, keep sentences short and to the point, state the purpose clearly and concisely, give relevant details. Format: Sender's address, Date, The Municipal Councillor (designation and address), Subject, Dear Madam/Sir, Body (4 paragraphs: Drawing attention to problem / Causes and Consequences / Request and Suggestions / Conclusion), Yours sincerely, Signature.

question_bank.stem: Write a formal letter to the Municipal Councillor requesting installation of a rainwater harvesting unit in your ward.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: The text uses words like 'piece,' 'weather,' 'son,' and 'rain'. These words are the same as other words that are spelt differently—peace, whether, sun, and reign. Words that are pronounced the same as other words but differ in meaning or in spelling are called homophones. Now, complete the paragraph by filling in the correct option of homophones given in the brackets.

question_bank.stem: Fill in blank 1: The farmer looked at the sky and wondered ________________ (weather/whether) the clouds would finally bring rain.
Fill in blank 2: He sat down on the ground, breaking a small ________________ (piece/peace) of roti for his meal.
Fill in blank 3: The ________________ (plain/plane) fields stretched endlessly before him, completely dry.
Fill in blank 4: He thought about how he could ________________ (sell/cell) his last sack of grain.
Fill in blank 5: Without the rains, it didn't ________________ (seam/seem) possible to grow another crop this season.
Fill in blank 6: every farmer hopes for the next ________________ (rain/reign) by nature to bring life back to the land.

This year, he (iv) __________ (grow) legumes, which help restore the nutrients in the soil. tasks_verbatim.task_text: Study the highlighted determiners in sentences from the text. Learn the use of common determiners: Articles (a, an, the), Demonstrative (this, that, these, those), Possessive (my, our, your, his, her, its, their, one's), Definite Numeral (one, two, three…), Indefinite Numeral (some, any, no, all, much, many, few, less, several, little), Distributive (each, every, either, neither). Now, fill in the blanks by choosing the correct determiner from those given in the brackets.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Rest is very important for us to function in a proper manner. Even the Earth needs rest. This is known as fallowing. Read the facts given below related to fallowing: A fallow year refers to leaving the land without sowing for one, or several vegetative cycles. This is done to allow the land to recover its nutrients and retain moisture. It also helps to avoid spreading of diseases and controlling pests. The sustainable land management method has been applied for centuries, wherein a field is divided into two halves—one is planted with crops while the other remains unplanted. Then, the process is reversed in the following year.
Find out from your Vocational Education teacher about Agriculture as a vocation and other related vocations, such as horticulture, etc.

question_bank.stem: Find out about Agriculture and related vocations (horticulture, etc.) from your Vocational Education teacher.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: India's rich tradition of meteorological knowledge includes indigenous forecasting systems, grounded in ancient wisdom. References to rain and weather are found in numerous ancient texts, including the Rigveda, Shrimad Bhagavad, Parashara Samhita, Panini's Ashtadhyayi, and Meghmala. These texts offer insights into rainfall patterns and seasonal changes. Meteorological Services for Agriculture in India: The primary objective of the Agricultural Meteorology Division, India Meteorological Department (IMD) is to minimise the impact of adverse weather on crops. For detailed information, visit: https://mausam.imd.gov.in/responsive/servicesMetEnvironment.php
